name: Code Review Scenarios
description: You're reviewing a PR — can you spot the issue?

questions.question: What issue would you flag in this code review?
options: The component should destructure users with a default empty array to prevent crashes
Rendering list items without a wrapper fragment causes React hydration mismatch errors
Each <li> rendered inside .map() is missing a unique key prop needed for reconciliation
The user.name property access requires optional chaining to handle null values safely
questions.explanation: React requires a unique 'key' prop on elements rendered inside .map() to efficiently track list changes. Without it, React may re-render the entire list unnecessarily.
optionExplanations: While defensive defaults are good practice, the code won't crash from a missing default — the real bug is the missing key prop that breaks React's reconciliation.
Fragments don't cause hydration mismatches here — the list renders fine structurally, but React needs key props to track individual list items.
Optional chaining is a nice safeguard, but if user objects exist in the array, accessing .name won't throw — the missing key prop is the actual issue.

questions.question: What's the problem with this useEffect?
options: The useEffect callback should be defined as a named async function for proper teardown
The document.title assignment should use a ref to avoid triggering unwanted side effects
The useState initialization value should match the current document title on first render
The dependency array is empty but references count, so the title won't update on changes
questions.explanation: The effect uses 'count' but the dependency array is empty []. This means the title only updates on mount, not when count changes. Add 'count' to the dependency array.
optionExplanations: useEffect callbacks don't need to be async here since document.title is a synchronous assignment — no async teardown is needed.
Setting document.title directly is standard practice — refs aren't needed for DOM (Document Object Model) properties outside the React tree.
The initial useState value doesn't need to match the document title — the real issue is the effect not re-running when count changes.

questions.question: Why won't this trigger a re-render?
options: The array is mutated in place so setItems receives the same reference and skips re-render
The push method returns the new length instead of the array, causing setItems to store a number
Calling setItems inside a synchronous handler blocks React's reconciliation batching cycle
The useState initializer with an array literal creates a new reference on every render pass
questions.explanation: Calling items.push() mutates the existing array. Since setItems receives the same reference, React doesn't detect a change. Use setItems([...items, 'c']) to create a new array.
optionExplanations: While push() does return the new length, that value isn't passed to setItems — items (the mutated array) is. The real issue is reference equality.
React handles synchronous handlers fine and batches state updates automatically — the problem is the mutated array reference.
The array literal only runs once during initialization, not every render — useState stores the initial value and ignores it on subsequent renders.

questions.scenario:
<button onClick={() => handleClick(id)}>
  Delete
</button>
questions.question: What potential performance issue exists here?
options: The arrow function captures stale closure variables from the parent component scope
Inline handlers bypass React's synthetic event pooling mechanism and reduce performance
A new function reference is created each render, breaking memoization of child components
Passing a callback with arguments prevents proper event delegation on the DOM (Document Object Model) element
questions.explanation: The inline arrow function creates a new function reference on every render. If this button is in a memoized child component, it will break memoization. Use useCallback or extract the handler.
optionExplanations: Arrow functions in JSX capture the current closure correctly — stale closures are a useEffect/useCallback issue, not an inline handler issue.
React's synthetic event system works identically with inline and named handlers — event pooling is unrelated to how the handler is defined.
React handles event delegation internally regardless of whether the handler is inline or extracted — this doesn't affect delegation behavior.

questions.question: What critical issue is in this useEffect?
options: The subscription is never cleaned up on unmount, causing a memory leak and stale updates
The subscribe callback should use the functional form of setMessages to avoid stale state
Placing an async subscription inside useEffect without await causes unhandled rejections
The dependency array should include api and setMessages to track all external references
questions.explanation: The subscription is never cleaned up. When the component unmounts, the callback still fires, causing memory leaks and state updates on unmounted components. Return a cleanup: return () => sub.unsubscribe();
optionExplanations: Using functional updates is good practice for avoiding stale state, but it's not the critical issue — the missing cleanup causes memory leaks.
The subscribe method here returns a subscription object synchronously — it's not a Promise, so await isn't needed or applicable.
setMessages is stable (from useState) and api is typically a module-level import — neither needs to be in the dependency array.

questions.question: What TypeScript issue would you flag?
options: The function return type should be explicitly annotated as Promise<User> for contracts
Template literal URL construction should use a URL builder to prevent type-unsafe paths
The res variable should be typed as Response instead of relying on implicit inference
Using 'any' annotations on all types removes the type safety that TypeScript provides
questions.explanation: Using 'any' everywhere removes all type safety. Define an interface for the user, type the parameter as string/number, and type the response. Also, res.json() needs 'await'.
optionExplanations: An explicit return type is a nice-to-have, but the bigger problem is that every parameter and variable uses 'any', eliminating all type safety.
Template literals for URL construction are common and acceptable — the core issue is the pervasive 'any' types removing all TypeScript benefits.
While typing res as Response is slightly better, fetch() already returns Response by default — the real problem is all the explicit 'any' annotations.

questions.question: What CSS code smell is present?
options: Cascading !important declarations create specificity conflicts that are very hard to maintain
The selectors use increasingly complex combinators that reduce overall CSS parsing performance
Named color values like red, blue, and green render inconsistently across different browsers
Each class override should be scoped inside a CSS module to prevent global style leakage
questions.explanation: When !important is used on every rule, you end up fighting specificity with more specificity. Refactor to use a clear, low-specificity naming convention like BEM (Block Element Modifier) instead.
optionExplanations: CSS selector complexity has negligible performance impact in modern browsers — the maintainability nightmare of cascading !important is far more concerning.
Named colors like red, blue, and green render consistently across modern browsers — the real issue is the !important specificity war.
CSS modules are one possible solution, but the core code smell is the !important abuse, which would still be problematic even inside a module.

questions.question: What's missing from this async function?
options: The function should validate the response status code before attempting to parse JSON
The setData call should be wrapped in startTransition to prevent UI jank on slow networks
There is no try/catch or error handling, so a failed request silently breaks the UI state
The fetch call needs an AbortController signal to cancel the request on component unmount
questions.explanation: There's no try/catch or .catch() handling. If the network request fails or returns a non-OK status, the error is unhandled and the UI may break silently. Always add error handling for async operations.
optionExplanations: Status code validation is important but secondary — without any error handling at all, even network failures will silently break the UI state.
startTransition is for deprioritizing non-urgent UI updates — it doesn't address the fundamental lack of error handling in this function.
AbortController is useful for cleanup on unmount, but it's an optimization — the missing error handling is a more critical omission.

questions.scenario:
<App>
  <Layout user={user}>
    <Sidebar user={user}>
      <NavMenu user={user}>
        <Avatar user={user} />
      </NavMenu>
    </Sidebar>
  </Layout>
</App>
questions.question: What architectural issue does this component tree show?
options: The deeply nested component tree slows down React's reconciliation and diffing passes
Each component should fetch its own user data instead of relying on a passed-down prop
Layout and Sidebar should be combined into a single compound component to reduce nesting
The user prop is drilled through every level even though intermediary components don't use it
questions.explanation: The 'user' prop is drilled through 4 levels. Use React Context, composition (children pattern), or a state management library to avoid passing props through intermediary components that don't use them.
optionExplanations: React handles nested component trees efficiently — depth alone doesn't significantly impact reconciliation performance.
Having each component fetch its own data would create redundant API (Application Programming Interface) calls — the issue is how the data is passed, not where it's fetched.
Combining Layout and Sidebar would reduce flexibility without solving the core problem of passing user through every level.

questions.question: What accessibility issues are present?
options: The SVG element inside the button should have a role attribute defining its semantic purpose
The image is missing alt text and the icon-only button has no accessible label for screen readers
Using lowercase onclick instead of onClick prevents the handler from binding correctly in JSX
The button element needs an explicit tabIndex attribute to be reachable via keyboard navigation
questions.explanation: The needs an alt attribute for screen readers. The icon-only button has no accessible label — add aria-label='Buy' so assistive technologies can describe its purpose.
optionExplanations: While SVG roles can help, the button itself needs an accessible label — a role on the SVG alone doesn't provide a usable label for assistive tech.
While onclick vs onClick is a valid JSX concern, it's a syntax issue, not an accessibility issue — the missing alt and label are more critical.
Buttons are natively keyboard-focusable without tabIndex — the real accessibility gaps are the missing alt text and button label.

questions.question: What's wrong with this async function?
options: The fetch call is missing the await keyword so response is a Promise, not a Response object
The function should use XMLHttpRequest instead of fetch for proper error status handling
The async keyword is unnecessary here since the function already returns a Promise value
The response.json() call should be replaced with JSON.parse(response.body) for safety
questions.explanation: Without 'await' before fetch(), response is a Promise, not a Response. Calling .json() on a Promise fails silently or throws. Always use 'await' with async operations inside async functions.
optionExplanations: fetch is the modern standard for HTTP requests — XMLHttpRequest is legacy and doesn't solve the missing await issue.
The async keyword is needed to use await inside the function — removing it would make the missing await even harder to fix.
JSON.parse is for strings, not Response objects — response.json() is correct once you actually await the fetch call first.

questions.question: What bug would you flag in this code review?
options: The showToast call runs immediately without waiting for the fetch to succeed or fail first
The POST body needs a Content-Type header set to application/json for proper server parsing
The function should use PUT instead of POST since it updates an existing settings resource
The JSON.stringify call should include a replacer function to filter sensitive user fields
questions.explanation: The fetch call is not awaited, so showToast('Settings saved!') runs immediately regardless of whether the request succeeds. The user sees a success message even if the save fails. Add 'await' and wrap in try/catch.
optionExplanations: A Content-Type header is good practice, but the core bug is that the success message shows before the request completes or fails.
PUT vs POST is a semantic choice that depends on the backend design — the missing await causing false success feedback is the real bug.
Filtering sensitive fields is a valid concern, but the function falsely reports success regardless of the request outcome.

questions.question: What TypeScript issue would you flag in this code?
options: The non-null assertion operator (!) bypasses null checks and will crash at runtime on null values
The reduce callback should use a generic type parameter to ensure the accumulator is typed
The union types should use a type guard instead of a union to enable narrowing in branches
The return types should be explicitly annotated as string and number for clarity in the API (Application Programming Interface)
questions.explanation: The non-null assertion operator (!) tells TypeScript to trust that the value is not null/undefined, but it provides no runtime protection. If user or order is actually null, the code throws a runtime error. Use proper null checks or optional chaining instead.
optionExplanations: The reduce accumulator type is inferred correctly from the initial value — generic type parameters aren't needed here.
Union types with null/undefined are standard TypeScript patterns — type guards work with them, but the real issue is the unsafe non-null assertions.
While explicit return types are nice, they don't fix the runtime crash caused by asserting non-null on potentially null values.

questions.question: What TypeScript and logic issue is present?
options: The function has no parameter types or return type, and the map returns undefined for non-active items
The spread operator inside map creates shallow copies that still share nested reference values
The input parameter should be typed as ReadonlyArray to prevent accidental mutation in the body
The equality check should use strict type narrowing with a discriminated union instead of strings
questions.explanation: The function lacks TypeScript types entirely — no parameter type, no return type. Additionally, the .map() callback only returns a value when item.type is 'active'. For all other items, it implicitly returns undefined, creating holes in the result array.
optionExplanations: Shallow copies from spread are standard practice for immutable updates — deep cloning is rarely needed for state transformations.
ReadonlyArray is a nice defensive pattern, but the critical issues are the missing types and the implicit undefined returns from map.
String comparisons for type checking are common and valid — discriminated unions help but don't fix the missing types or undefined returns.

questions.question: What security vulnerability exists in this component?
options: The query parameter rendered with dangerouslySetInnerHTML enables XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks
The results array lacks input validation which could allow injected objects with malicious properties
The component should sanitize the query string by encoding special HTML characters before display
The key prop using r.id could expose internal database identifiers to end users via the markup
questions.explanation: Using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-provided input (the search query) is a textbook XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) vulnerability. An attacker could craft a query like <script>stealCookies()</script> that executes in every user's browser. Use text content rendering instead: {query}.
optionExplanations: React automatically escapes text content in JSX — the results rendering is safe because it uses {r.title} which doesn't allow HTML injection.
Encoding is one mitigation, but the simplest fix is to not use dangerouslySetInnerHTML at all — just render the query as text with {query}.
Key props are used by React internally and aren't typically exposed in a way that creates a meaningful security risk to end users.

questions.question: What critical issue would you flag in this code?
options: Using eval() with external input allows arbitrary code execution and is a severe security risk
Dynamic style objects should use CSS custom properties instead of inline styles for performance
The template literal wrapping is incorrect and will throw a SyntaxError on complex expressions
The config parameter should be validated with a schema library before accessing its properties
questions.explanation: eval() executes arbitrary JavaScript code. If config.styleExpression comes from user input or an external source, an attacker can run any code in the user's browser. Never use eval() with dynamic content — parse the style configuration with a safe parser instead.
optionExplanations: CSS custom properties are useful but don't address the security vulnerability — eval with external input is dangerous regardless of the styling approach.
The template literal syntax may work for simple cases, but the fundamental problem is using eval at all, not the specific syntax wrapper.
Schema validation is good practice but insufficient — even validated strings passed to eval can contain malicious code if the schema isn't perfect.

questions.question: What testing anti-pattern is demonstrated here?
options: The test relies on test IDs instead of accessible roles, coupling it to implementation details
Using fireEvent instead of userEvent means the interactions don't simulate real user behavior
The mock assertion checks exact call arguments instead of verifying visible UI feedback to users
The test lacks an async act() wrapper that is required for state updates triggered by events
questions.explanation: Using getByTestId couples the test to implementation details (specific data-testid attributes). If the component restructures its markup, tests break even though behavior is unchanged. Use getByRole('textbox', { name: /email/i }) and getByRole('button') to query by accessible roles, making tests more resilient and verifying accessibility.
optionExplanations: While userEvent is preferred for more realistic interaction simulation, fireEvent still works — the bigger anti-pattern is the test ID coupling.
Checking mock call arguments is valid for verifying form submission — the problem is how the elements are queried, not what's asserted.
act() is automatically handled by render and fireEvent in most testing library setups — it's not the issue demonstrated here.

questions.question: What React rules violation is in this component?
options: Hooks are called inside conditional branches which violates the Rules of Hooks ordering contract
The fetchAdminData and fetchUserData calls should be combined into a single unified data fetcher
The useState initial value of null requires a type annotation to avoid implicit any in TypeScript
The conditional rendering with a ternary should use a Suspense boundary for the loading state
questions.explanation: React hooks must be called in the same order on every render. Placing useEffect inside an if/else means hooks are called conditionally, which breaks React's internal hook tracking. Instead, call one useEffect and put the condition inside it.
optionExplanations: Combining the fetchers is a design choice, not a bug — the critical issue is that hooks are called conditionally, breaking React's rules.
TypeScript typing is a best practice concern, but the conditional hooks cause a runtime React error that crashes the component entirely.
Suspense is for lazy-loaded components and data fetching libraries that support it — it doesn't fix the conditional hooks violation.

questions.question: What issue would you flag in this Timer component?
options: The setInterval callback should use seconds directly instead of the functional updater form
The interval is never cleared on unmount, causing a memory leak and updates to stale component
The useEffect dependency array should include seconds to re-register the interval each tick
Using setInterval in React is unreliable and should be replaced with requestAnimationFrame
questions.explanation: The setInterval is never cleared because the useEffect doesn't return a cleanup function. When the component unmounts, the interval continues firing, causing a memory leak and potential 'setState on unmounted component' warnings. Return a cleanup: return () => clearInterval(id);
optionExplanations: The functional updater form (prev => prev + 1) is actually correct here — it avoids stale closure issues with the interval callback.
Adding seconds to the dependency array would clear and recreate the interval every second, causing drift — the functional updater avoids this.
setInterval works reliably in React when properly cleaned up — requestAnimationFrame is for animation frames, not fixed-interval timers.

questions.question: Why does this function return an empty array?
options: Array.forEach does not await async callbacks, so the function returns before any fetches complete
The results array should be declared with const but initialized using Array.from for async use
The fetchOrder function needs to be wrapped in Promise.resolve to work inside the forEach loop
The push method is not safe to call concurrently and needs a mutex or synchronization wrapper
questions.explanation: Array.forEach ignores the return value of its callback, including Promises. The async callbacks start executing but forEach doesn't await them. The function returns the empty results array immediately. Use for...of with await, or Promise.all with .map() instead.
optionExplanations: The array declaration style has no effect on async behavior — const with [] is fine. The issue is forEach not awaiting the callbacks.
fetchOrder already returns a Promise — wrapping it in Promise.resolve is redundant and doesn't fix forEach ignoring async results.
JavaScript is single-threaded so push doesn't need synchronization — the problem is that forEach returns before any async callbacks resolve.
